incorrectExplanations.c: Direct browser calls expose Bedrock credentials. All AI calls should go through backend services.
incorrectExplanations.d: Full replacement is expensive, risky, and unnecessary when you can incrementally add AI features.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Enhancing existing apps with GenAI = Lambda behind existing API Gateway

questions.examTip: Add GenAI as new endpoints/functions behind existing APIs. Don't create separate systems or replace what works.

questions.scenario: A development team wants AI assistance for understanding unfamiliar codebases, generating unit tests, and explaining complex code. They use VS Code and want the AI to have context about the current file and project.
questions.question: Which tool provides AI-powered code assistance integrated with VS Code?

options.text: Amazon Q Business configured with code repository connectors
Amazon Q Developer with VS Code extension
Amazon CodeCatalyst for project management
AWS Cloud9 with built-in AI features

questions.explanation: Amazon Q Developer (formerly CodeWhisperer) is specifically designed for developers. The VS Code extension provides code-aware AI assistance including explanations, test generation, and code completion with full project context.

incorrectExplanations.a: Q Business is for enterprise knowledge assistants, not IDE-integrated code assistance.
incorrectExplanations.c: CodeCatalyst is for project management and CI/CD, not code assistance within the IDE.
incorrectExplanations.d: Cloud9 is a cloud IDE but doesn't have the same code assistance capabilities as Q Developer.

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: AI-powered code assistance in VS Code
eliminationHints: Q Business = enterprise knowledge, not code
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: IDE code assistance = Q Developer

questions.examTip: Q Developer is for developers in IDEs. Q Business is for business users with enterprise knowledge.

questions.scenario: An AI agent for travel booking needs to search flights, check hotel availability, and process payments. The team is defining the agent's Action Groups and wants to follow best practices.
questions.question: Which TWO practices should guide Action Group design?

options.text: Group related actions together (all flight operations in one Action Group, hotel in another)
Define clear OpenAPI schemas for each action so the agent understands parameters and responses
Create one Action Group per API endpoint for maximum granularity
Avoid Action Groups entirely and use direct Lambda invocation

questions.explanation: Best practices: (1) Group related actions logically (flight operations together) for cleaner agent reasoning. (2) Clear OpenAPI schemas help the agent understand what each action does and what parameters it needs. This improves action selection accuracy.

incorrectExplanations.c: One Action Group per endpoint creates too many groups, making agent reasoning more complex. Logical grouping is better.
incorrectExplanations.d: Direct Lambda invocation bypasses the agent's action selection capabilities. Action Groups are how agents interface with tools.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Action Group design = logical grouping + clear OpenAPI schemas

questions.examTip: Action Groups should be logically grouped with clear OpenAPI definitions. Too many groups complicates agent reasoning.

questions.scenario: A production GenAI system experiences occasional model timeouts during peak hours. The team wants requests to succeed even when the primary model is slow or unavailable.
questions.question: Which resilience pattern handles model unavailability without failing requests?

options.text: Increase all timeout values to wait longer for responses
Implement circuit breaker pattern with fallback to a secondary model or cached responses
Add more retry attempts with the same model
Queue requests and process them when the model recovers

questions.explanation: Circuit breakers detect degraded service and stop sending requests to failing systems. Combined with fallback (secondary model or cache), requests succeed with slightly reduced capability rather than failing entirely. This is the core resilience pattern.

incorrectExplanations.a: Longer timeouts just increase latency. If the model is consistently slow, users wait longer without benefit.
incorrectExplanations.c: Retrying a failing model wastes resources and doesn't improve success rate when the model is overloaded.
incorrectExplanations.d: Queuing provides eventual consistency but doesn't provide immediate responses to users.

eliminationHints: More retries = wastes resources on failing model
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Model resilience = circuit breaker + fallback

questions.examTip: Circuit breaker pattern detects failures and redirects to fallbacks. It prevents cascading failures and provides graceful degradation.
